message.abstract:
<jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>CoVid-19 can be a life-threatening lung disease or a trivial upper respiratory infection depending on whether the alveoli are involved. Emergency department (ED) screening in symptomatic patients with normal vital signs is frequently limited to oro-nasopharyngeal swabs. We tested the null hypothesis that patients being screened for CoVid-19 in the ED with normal vital signs and without hypoxia would have a point-of-care lung ultrasound (LUS) consistent with CoVid-19 less than 2% of the time.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Subjects</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Subjects were identified from ED ultrasound logs.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Inclusion criteria</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Age 14 years or older with symptoms prompting ED screening for CoVid-19.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Exclusion criteria</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Known congestive heart failure or other chronic lung condition likely to cause excessive B lines on LUS.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Intervention</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Structured blinded ultrasound review and chart review</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Analysis</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>We used a two-sided exact hypothesis test for binomial random variables. We also measured LUS diagnostic performance using computed tomography as the gold standard.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>We reviewed 77 charts; 49 met inclusion criteria. Vital signs were normal in 30/49 patients; 10 (33%) of these patients had LUS consistent with CoVid-19. We rejected the null hypothesis (p-value < 0.001). The treating physicians’ interpretation of their own point of care lung ultrasounds had a sensitivity of 100% (95% CI 75%, 100%) and specificity of 80% (95% CI 68%, 89%).</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>LUS has a meaningful detection rate for CoVid-19 in symptomatic ED patients with normal vital signs. We recommend at least LUS be used in addition to PCR testing when screening symptomatic ED patients for CoVid-19.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s9">
                  <jats:title>Capsule</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec id="s9a">
                    <jats:title>What is known</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Auscultation and chest x-ray are insufficient to screen for lung involvement when SARS-CoV-2 infection is suspected. Point of care lung ultrasound is widely available, safer, and less resource intensive than CT imaging.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec id="s9b">
                    <jats:title>What we found</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>In symptomatic patients presenting to the ED even those with normal vital signs had point of care lung ultrasound evidence of alveolar level involvement 33%of patients. Point of care lung ultrasound was 100% sensitive and 80% specific compared to CT (reference standard) when evaluating patients for Covid-19.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec id="s9c">
                    <jats:title>What this adds</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Point of care lung ultrasound or similar imaging should performed when screening symptomatic patients in whom SARS-CoV-2 infection is suspected.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Point of care lung ultrasound is useful when screening for CoVid-19 in Emergency Department patients
